topic: Part 5: Kill-Shot Binary Tests — post-ISS-860/861/862 patch review (fresh adversarial pass)

queue_item.reason: ISS-860/861/862 applied: fixed fabricated 30% threshold in verdict paragraph, deleted duplicate sentence, corrected dome-says-PASS opening. Re-attack with fresh eyes.

prior_review: SEC-5-post-EXP082.c3.json (2026-04-13T14:30:00Z) — found 1 critical (fabricated 30% threshold), 1 major (duplicate sentence), 3 moderate, 2 minor. ISS-860/861/862 targeted the critical and major issues plus the moderate opening-paragraph mischaracterization.

patch_verification.ISS-860_30pct_threshold: FIXED. Verdict item (b) now reads: 'approaches the dome's own >50% falsification threshold (which requires >50% error for 3 consecutive years — a criterion so generous that nearly-half-wrong predictions still pass).' The fabricated 30% has been removed.
patch_verification.ISS-861_duplicate_sentence: FIXED. Only one instance of 'The dome's own tracking shows substantial error.' remains in the Test 6 body paragraph. The duplicate 'shows failure' sentence has been deleted.
patch_verification.ISS-862_opening_paragraph: FIXED. Opening now reads: 'one (NMP drift rate) self-reports as passing at 39.9% error — though only by widening the tolerance to 50% after predictions missed.' Accurately represents the dome's position while conveying the critique.

our_argument_summary: Part 5 presents six dome-defined kill-shot binary tests plus one we add (Gaia parallax). The three ISS patches resolved the most exploitable vulnerabilities from the c3 review: the fabricated 30% threshold, the duplicate sentence, and the mischaracterized opening. The NMP analysis now has internally consistent numbers (39.9% ≈ 40.0%, >50% threshold verified against dome's raw text). The core argument — the dome's kill-shot framework favors dome falsification by its own rules — is sound. However, fresh review reveals two new issues: (1) Test 1 TLDR attributes V12's 78% Sydney-Buenos Aires error to V13, contradicting the detail text; (2) the NMP '<20 km/yr by 2027' target cannot be found in the dome's published material and the dome's own formula gives 21 km/yr for 2027 (crosses <20 in mid-2028).

holes_found.description: Test 1 TLDR says 'The V13 Finsler formula...fails by 78% when tested on Sydney–Buenos Aires.' The detail text correctly states 'The V12 Finsler formula produced a −78% error on this route...V13 claims to have reduced this to −8.4%.' The TLDR attributes V12's error to V13 — the current version. A dome defender will immediately say: 'Your summary claims my V13 formula fails by 78% but your own detail text admits I fixed this and V13 is at 8.4%. Did you even read your own analysis?' This is a TLDR-contradicts-detail mismatch, which the curmudgeon prompt rates as major. Additionally, the body text calls it 'V12 Finsler formula' but Finsler was introduced in V13 (per poller change log 2026-04-05); V12 uses a simpler exponential H(r) coordinate system. So 'V12 Finsler' is itself a misnomer.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the TLDR to: 'The V13 Finsler formula was calibrated against the known Indian Pacific railway distance — that is curve-fitting, not prediction. Its predecessor (V12) failed by 78% on Sydney–Buenos Aires; V13 claims −8.4% via unpublished scaling parameters. Two internal methods (Finsler vs MDS scaffold) disagree by 460 km on Sydney–Perth itself.' In the body, change 'V12 Finsler formula' to 'V12 coordinate system' (since V12 didn't use Finsler).

holes_found.description: The NMP body text opens with 'The dome predicts the North Magnetic Pole (NMP) drift rate will decelerate to <20 km/yr by 2027.' The TLDR uses this target: 'Reaching the target of <20 km/yr by 2027 would require ~18 km/yr/yr deceleration.' Two problems: (1) This claim cannot be found in the dome's published material (flagged in c3, still unresolved). The dome's formula (rate = 55×exp(−0.08×(year−2015))) gives 21.06 km/yr for 2027 — above 20. The formula doesn't cross <20 until mid-2028 (year ≈ 2027.65). (2) If the target is our derivation from the dome's formula, it's wrong — the formula gives 21, not <20, for 2027. We may be critiquing a prediction the dome never made, which is exactly the strawmanning we accuse the dome of in Tests 3, 4, and 5.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) Source the '<20 km/yr by 2027' claim to a specific dome publication and cite it, or (b) replace with the dome's actual formula prediction: 'The dome's formula predicts ~21 km/yr for 2027 and ~19 km/yr for 2028, while the observed rate is ~38 km/yr — an error already at 40%.' The TLDR deceleration computation should be removed or reframed: the current 40% error is the damning number, not a fabricated future target. The dome's formula is already demonstrably failing without us inventing a more aggressive claim to attack.

holes_found.description: Closing summary says 'One test is failing by nearly 40%' — but the opening paragraph (corrected by ISS-862) accurately states the dome 'self-reports as passing at 39.9% error.' The closing reverts to unqualified 'failing' language. A dome defender can quote the opening against the closing: 'Your opening says I report PASS, your closing says I'm failing — which is it?' The test has 40% error but the dome counts it as PASS. Our argument should be 'the dome counts this as PASS despite 40% error' (asymmetric scorecard), not 'it's failing' (which the dome disputes).
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite to: 'One test shows 39.9% error — counted as PASS only via a widened tolerance and a three-year consecutive requirement that allows single-year escapes.' This maintains the critique without contradicting the opening's accurate characterization.

holes_found.description: Closing summary characterizes Polaris as 'within measurement noise' but omits the strongest Test 2 argument: the dome's own site marks the broader Polaris-latitude prediction as FALSIFIED (FAIL-001). This was flagged in c3 as a residual and remains unfixed. The self-falsification is more devastating than the noise-floor argument because it comes from the dome's own scoring system, making it immune to defender rebuttal about our measurement uncertainty estimates.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to closing: 'within measurement noise (Polaris: claimed excess smaller than the star's own polar offset, and the dome's broader Polaris-latitude prediction is self-marked FALSIFIED).' This invokes the dome's own admission, which is the strongest available argument.

holes_found.description: Strength rating key states 'Current ratings: 3 decisive, 2 major, 2 supportive' but individual test ratings are not visible in the text. The reader cannot verify which tests received which rating. This makes the count an unsupported assertion.
holes_found.recommendation: Either add rating badges to individual test headers (e.g., 'Test 7 — Stellar Parallax (Gaia DR3) [DECISIVE]') or remove the count from the rating key and let readers draw their own conclusions.

holes_found.description: Test 5 (Eclipse 2026) remains a single-paragraph stub that defers entirely to Section 4.2. While understandable for a pending test, the disparity in treatment (Tests 1, 3, 6, 7 get multi-paragraph analysis; Test 5 gets one paragraph) makes the section feel incomplete. A dome defender reading the kill-shot page specifically would find the eclipse test — the one they're most invested in as a pending prediction — dismissed with the least effort.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand Test 5 to at least two paragraphs: one on the Chapman mechanism (already in Section 4.2) and one on the ephemeris dependency (eclipse path requires JPL DE440/441). This doesn't need to replicate Section 4.2 but should give the kill-shot reader enough to understand the argument without clicking through.

holes_found.description: Historical deceleration rate in TLDR: ~0.77 km/yr/yr is correct (peak ~55 km/yr circa 2003 to ~38 km/yr by 2025, over ~22 years). The ratio of 23× is also correct (18/0.77). However, 'peak ~55 km/yr circa 2003' is approximate — Livermore et al. (2020) documents peak drift speed of ~60 km/yr in 2003-2005. Using 60 km/yr: (60-38)/22 = 1.0 km/yr/yr, giving a ratio of 18×. The TLDR's ~0.77 uses our 55 km/yr estimate, producing a more dramatic ratio than the peer-reviewed 60 km/yr figure would. This favors us but isn't the most defensible number.
holes_found.recommendation: Use the Livermore et al. (2020) figure if available: peak ~60 km/yr, giving historical deceleration of ~1.0 km/yr/yr and ratio of ~18×. This is less dramatic but more defensible and matches the peer-reviewed literature. Alternatively, cite both: 'roughly 18–23× the historical average (depending on peak estimate used).'

stronger_arguments.description: The asymmetric scorecard argument from the c3 review deserves integration into the closing summary. The dome uses 50% tolerance for NMP (where it's losing) but much tighter thresholds elsewhere: Schumann >0.3 Hz drift for >1 hour (never observed in recorded history), equatorial gravity >0.1% vs WGS84, and SAA Decay at 20%. Side-by-side comparison makes the systematic bias explicit and is harder for a defender to dismiss than any single test critique.

Test 1 should emphasize that the V13 -8.4% claim is unverifiable because the key scaling function n(r) is not published. This is actually stronger than citing V12's 78% error. 'The dome claims improvement but won't show the formula' is a more damning argument than 'the old version failed' — because the dome can always say 'I fixed it.'
sources: The body text already makes this point but the TLDR should lead with it

kernel_of_truth.description: The ISS-860/861/862 patches were well-targeted surgical fixes that resolved the three most exploitable vulnerabilities from the c3 review. The 30% → >50% correction in the verdict, the duplicate sentence deletion, and the opening paragraph rewrite all land correctly. Post-patch, the NMP Test 6 analysis is internally consistent on the numbers that matter most: 39.9% ≈ 40.0% error, >50% falsification criterion, formula = 22.8 km/yr for 2026. The core kill-shot framework argument (dome's own binary rule favors dome falsification) is structurally sound and rhetorically effective.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The patches fixed the ISS-targeted items but did not address the broader residuals from c3 (Polaris self-falsification omission, Test 5 stub, NMP target unverifiability). Additionally, fresh review uncovered the Test 1 V12/V13 version misattribution in the TLDR, which is a new major hole created by the V13 references being added without updating the summary claim. The section is much stronger than pre-ISS but still has two major exploitable points.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender reading post-patch Part 5 would construct: (1) 'Your Test 1 summary says my V13 formula fails by 78% on Sydney-Buenos Aires. Your own body text says V12 had the 78% error and V13 is at 8.4%. You're citing superseded results in your headline summary while burying the current version's improvement in the fine print — exactly the kind of selective framing you accuse me of.' (2) 'You claim I predict <20 km/yr by 2027, but my formula gives 21 km/yr for 2027 — you can check it yourself: 55×exp(-0.08×12) = 21.06. You fabricated a target I never stated, then computed an implausible deceleration rate needed to hit your fabricated target. This is a straw man.' (3) 'Test 7 is YOUR addition, not one of MY six kill-shot tests. My framework has six tests; two confirmed, three pending, one passing. You can't fail my framework by adding your own test and then citing your result.' (4) 'Your closing says one test is failing. Your opening says I report PASS. Make up your mind.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Points (1) and (2) are genuinely exploitable and require text fixes. Point (3) is weaker — we can defend adding Test 7 by noting the dome's framework implies stellar parallax via WIN-017, and a genuinely falsifiable framework should welcome additional tests. Point (4) is a framing inconsistency easily fixed. After fixes, defense_survives drops to 1 (only the Test 7 'our addition' objection survives, and it's easily countered).

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No new cross-WIN inconsistencies found post-ISS patches. The Polaris offset range (0.66–0.74° in Test 2 vs ~0.63° actual 2026 value) persists as a known minor discrepancy from c3. WIN-017 (stellar parallax) is correctly cross-referenced in Test 7. WIN-027 (southern distance quadratic) relates to Test 1's V13 coordinate system claims but is not cross-referenced — the V13 Finsler formula's Sydney-Perth prediction may conflict with or supersede WIN-027's distance model. This should be checked.

claims_checked: Dome falsification criterion: >50% for 3 consecutive years ✓ (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 65)
Dome formula for 2027: 55×exp(-0.96) = 21.06 km/yr (ABOVE 20, not below)
Dome formula crosses <20 at year ≈ 2027.65 (mid-2028, not 2027)
quantitative_verification.computations: Python verification of all numerical claims. NMP formula, error calculation, historical deceleration, Gaia parallax, and sigma calculations all match the published text. KEY DISCREPANCY: The claim '<20 km/yr by 2027' is contradicted by the dome's own formula, which gives 21.06 km/yr for 2027. The formula doesn't cross <20 until approximately August 2028 (year ≈ 2027.65). The TLDR's deceleration computation (18 km/yr/yr needed, 23× historical) is based on reaching <20 from 38 in 1 year — but the dome's formula doesn't even reach <20 in its own output for 2027.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) '<20 km/yr by 2027' — dome's formula gives 21 km/yr for 2027; target appears fabricated. (2) Test 1 TLDR '78%' attributed to V13 but body says V12. (3) 'V12 Finsler formula' misnomer — Finsler introduced in V13, V12 uses simpler coordinate system.

recommended_action_detail: Two major fixes needed: (1) Test 1 TLDR — replace '78%' V13 claim with accurate V12/V13 distinction. (2) NMP — either source '<20 km/yr by 2027' to dome publication or replace with formula-accurate prediction (~21 km/yr for 2027). Two moderate fixes: (3) Closing 'failing' → 'shows 39.9% error'. (4) Add Polaris FAIL-001 to closing. Three minor fixes for strength ratings, Test 5 stub, and historical deceleration sourcing. The ISS patches successfully resolved the c3 critical/major issues; no urgent structural rewrites needed.

citations_verified: Dome NMP falsification criterion: '>50% for 3 consecutive years' — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 65
Dome NMP formula: rate = 55×exp(-0.08×(year-2015)) — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 64
Dome dashboard: 'PASS (39.9% error)' — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt
V13 Finsler introduction date: 2026-04-05 — confirmed in monitor/changes/2026-04-05T18-24-13Z.json
Livermore et al. (2020) Nature Geoscience — valid for NMP acceleration documentation
Dome parallax mechanism: 20 m firmament wobble at H=9,086 km → 0.454 arcsec — consistent with dome model parameters
citations_failed: '<20 km/yr by 2027' — NOT FOUND in dome's published material. Dome formula gives 21 km/yr for 2027. This target may be our interpolation of the dome's exponential formula, but it's inaccurate even as an interpolation.
Test 1 TLDR '78%' attributed to V13 — V12 had the 78% error (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt: 'Cross-equator: 25-78% on long pairs'). V13 claims -8.4% per dome's own site update.
'V12 Finsler formula' — V12 uses H(r) exponential coordinate system, not Finsler. Finsler metric introduced in V13.
citations_unchecked: Terra-Nova et al. (2017, PNAS) — cited in Test 4 for SAA two-cell documentation; plausible but not verified against PNAS archives
Lauer et al. 2020 — cited in Test 7 for New Horizons parallax measurement; plausible reference (note: queue item 98 separately fixed 'Throop et al. 2020' → 'Lauer et al. 2020')
van Leeuwen 2007 — cited for Hipparcos parallax values; specific values for Betelgeuse (4.51 mas) may be from Harper et al. 2017 radio interferometry rather than Hipparcos revised catalog
Williams 2016 Environ. Res. Lett. — cited for seasonal JFK-LHR asymmetry figures; unverified from c3
